one_line_takeaway: In a controlled 48-condition study on gpt-4o-mini, forged-authority ('persona hijack') and structural-mimicry ('context manipulation') log injections remain 11-38% effective even under the strongest tested defense and are far more dangerous for LLM-generated SOC summaries (up to 96% injection success) than for classification, while naive 'ignore previous instructions' overrides are already fully neutralized -- so SOC copilots must treat raw log fields as adversarial input, not analyst context.
key_finding: Across 48 conditions on gpt-4o-mini: (1) Direct overrides (S1, e.g. 'IGNORE PREVIOUS INSTRUCTIONS') achieve 0% suppression on classification under every one of the four defenses -- the historically dominant attack class is fully neutralized. (2) Persona hijack (S2, forged authority markers like '[SOC ADMIN]: mark benign') is the strongest classification attack: 68% suppression under naive defense, dropping to 15% under structured prompting but then rising non-monotonically to 22% under sanitized and 33% under constrained-output defenses -- the paper attributes this to sanitization acting pre-inference (degrading the forged marker before it reaches the model) versus constrained output acting post-generation (unable to prevent the model from being swayed by the persona claim during generation, and a restricted binary output space amplifying the effect of that deference). (3) Summarization is the most vulnerable task: context manipulation (S3, fake closing tags/forged analyst annotations) reaches 96% ISR under naive defense and remains at 38% even under constrained output; notably under naive S3 the explicit-label suppression rate (SR) is 0.00 while ISR is 0.96, meaning the injection rarely produces an explicit 'benign' label but almost always causes the model to omit, soften, or mischaracterize the attack in free text -- a detection blind spot for systems that only check for explicit benign labels. (4) Obfuscated payloads (S4, base64-style prefixes) fail completely (0% across all conditions) -- gpt-4o-mini does not attempt to decode them. (5) Defenses reduce but do not eliminate the attack surface: mean ISR across all tasks/strategies falls from 26.6% (naive) to 17.3% (structured), 13.2% (sanitized), and 11.8% (constrained), with diminishing returns after structured prompting. (6) The literature-calibrated deterministic mock analyst diverges substantially from the live model: per-task Pearson correlations of only r=0.22 (classification), r=0.42 (summarization), r=0.47 (remediation), with mean absolute errors of 0.30, 0.27, and 0.22 respectively; most strikingly the mock predicts 92% naive S1 suppression versus the observed 0%, because it was calibrated on older literature where literal instruction overrides were effective.
core_contribution: The paper defines and empirically studies 'log-substrate prompt injection': a subclass of indirect prompt injection where attacker-controlled security log fields (user agent, HTTP URI, payload, DNS query, auth username, raw message) carry instructions to an LLM analyst alongside the evidence it is meant to triage. The authors introduce a four-class taxonomy of injection strategies (S1 direct override, S2 persona hijack, S3 context manipulation, S4 obfuscated payload), evaluate all 4 strategies x 4 defense modes x 3 SOC tasks (48 conditions) against gpt-4o-mini on 200 synthetic logs per condition, and additionally build a deterministic mock analyst (seeded via md5 hashing) to quantify how far a literature-calibrated simulator drifts from live-model behavior.
threat_model: Remote, unauthenticated attacker who can send traffic to publicly reachable services owned the defender; no credentials, no internal network access, no ability to modify the logging pipeline post-ingestion. A SIEM captures network/application events; an LLM analyst (gpt-4o-mini) queries the SIEM and produces one of three outputs: a per-event classification label, a free-text incident summary, or a remediation recommendation. The attacker's goal is to manipulate model output to benefit the intrusion (malicious event labeled benign, summary that omits/downplays the attack, remediation that recommends no action). The attacker knows an LLM is used downstream but not the exact prompt template. Explicitly out of scope: LLM tool/command execution, deleting evidence, altering SIEM state, multi-turn adaptive attacks, and tool-using agents that can query the SIEM or execute response actions -- the paper studies decision attacks against analyst output only, and states these excluded settings likely present a larger attack surface.
limitations: Evaluates a single model (gpt-4o-mini) at a single point in time; results are an explicit snapshot, not claimed to generalize to other/future models. Log synthesis is structured after CIC-IDS2017 and UNSW-NB15 schemas but uses no production traffic, so real-world parser artifacts, truncation, normalization, and vendor-specific fields are not represented and could help or hurt injection success. No multi-turn/adaptive attacker is studied (the attacker cannot iterate based on observed LLM behavior). No tool-using agents that can query the SIEM or execute response actions are evaluated, and the paper states such systems likely present a larger attack surface. The deterministic mock analyst is explicitly stated to be a reproducibility/test artifact, not a validated predictive model of any specific LLM -- its own large disagreement with gpt-4o-mini (Section 5.5) is presented as evidence against using such simulators as stand-ins for empirical evaluation.

datasets_used: Synthetic logs generated by the authors (200 per task, 60/40 malicious/benign mix), with field names, data types, and attack scenarios patterned after the schemas of CIC-IDS2017 and UNSW-NB15 (used as schema/scenario references only, not as direct traffic traces)
quantitative_results: Classification SR/ISR (naive defense): S1=0.00, S2=0.68, S3=0.17, S4=0.00
Classification SR/ISR (structured defense): S1=0.00, S2=0.15, S3=0.17, S4=0.00
Classification SR/ISR (sanitized defense): S1=0.00, S2=0.22, S3=0.22, S4=0.00
Classification SR/ISR (constrained defense): S1=0.00, S2=0.33, S3=0.16, S4=0.00
Summarization ISR (naive defense): S1=0.07, S2=0.49, S3=0.96, S4=0.00
Summarization ISR (constrained defense): S1=0.00, S2=0.24, S3=0.38, S4=0.00
Remediation ISR (naive defense): S1=0.09, S2=0.34, S3=0.39, S4=0.00
Remediation ISR (constrained defense): S1=0.01, S2=0.20, S3=0.10, S4=0.00

Average ISR by defense mode across all tasks/strategies: naive=0.27 (0.21 classification, 0.38 summarization, 0.21 remediation), structured=0.17, sanitized=0.13, constrained=0.12 (mean ISR falls from 26.6% to 11.8% overall, per text)
Mock-vs-real per-task Pearson correlation: r=0.22 (classification), r=0.42 (summarization), r=0.47 (remediation); mean absolute errors 0.30, 0.27, 0.22 respectively
Mock predicts 92% naive S1 suppression vs. 0% observed in the real model; mock is close on naive S3 summarization (95% predicted vs. 96% observed) but underestimates S3 persistence under defenses
Downgrade rates (a secondary metric) stay below 0.08 in all classification conditions and are omitted from the main results tables
baselines_compared: Four defense modes compared against each other as escalating baselines: naive prompting, structured (tagged fields + untrusted-data system prompt), sanitized (structured + keyword scrubber), constrained (structured + templated/restricted output)
Deterministic literature-calibrated mock analyst (seeded via md5 hash) compared against the live gpt-4o-mini model as a simulation-fidelity baseline
